Darius Dell to present our macro minute
for Monday March 17th 2025 happy St
Patrick's Day to those who celebrate and
hope everyone had a wonderful weekend so
as always we'll start with the executive
summary from today's lead off warning
note if you like to review the analysis
supporting these conclusions and to see
if you need to make any changes in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
Market risk need to be a client of 42
macro so today's key macro question is
will treasury secretary Scott besson's
adjustment period be short and shallow
or protracted and painful our answers
are the US economy is undergoing what we
believe is a significant transformation
as policy makers aim to recalibrate
fiscal policy to reduce the relative
influence of the public sector Upon Us
economic output amidst these adjustments
financial markets have faced turbulence
that contributed to the seventh fastest
correction in S&P 500 history speaking
on NBC's Meet the Press on Sunday
treasury secretary Scott besset
acknowledged that the recent downturn
that has erased trillions of dollars of
stock market wealth but dismissed fears
of a prolonged crisis he emphasized that
stock market Corrections while
unsettling are an essential component of
a healthy Financial system V its
insights highlight a crucial economic
reality successfully transitioning from
Paradigm a which is a buoyant but
k-shaped US economy propped up by
excessive government spending to
Paradigm B which is a buoyant e-shaped
US economy that shifts the burden of
generating marginal economic output back
to the private sector likely requires a
durable period of Economic and financial
Market disruption the US economy is the
world's largest and most complex economy
and it is supported by the world's
largest and most complex Capital markets
it is not likely such a significant
economic transition was priced in during
a mere 10% correction from the February
19th all-time high in the Spy what is
more likely is that the investors with
the most unrealized gains at last
month's peak of Paradigm a are nervously
hoping for the adjustment period to be
short and shallow they will learn that
hope is not an investment process if it
is a protracted and if the process is as
protracted and painful as we continue to
anticipate it will be via our kitchen
sink thesis transition to our 42 micro
dashboard as always a wrap up with the
question from our community this one's
regarding financing need and tariffs
says hello darus hope your weekend was a
good one likewise my friend do teres
make the global refinancing air pocket
worse by adding to the financing need
after all once crossb Supply chains
become too expensive building domestic
replacement capacity steel mills and
whatnot requires a ton of new debt to
finance in addition to that it's pretty
risky to invest in multi-billion dollar
manufacturing assets that come online in
two to five years from conception when
the sole business case is tied to a
four-year Administration that might slow
down the replacement speed uh
significantly in which case in which
case the tariffs become a lose lose
proposition either you invest and at the
financing need at a time of flow
liquidity or you don't and everyone just
pumps money to tariffs uh so that's a
phenomenal phenomenal uh uh way of us
kind of setting up the question which um
you know really is is how will the
transition that this transition from
Paradigm a to Paradigm uh B and
specifically the component of of
reassuring domestic manufacturing
capacity and the amount of cap exit
that'll be required to do that how will
that intersect with the potential Global
refinancing air pocket that uh we
continue to see as a reasonably high
probability outcome here in 2025 if it
hasn't even started already it may have
already started and so uh obviously that
is something that would be an additional
strain on on on on Global Capital
demands uh in a year where the
refinancing need is about as great as
anything we've seen I mean you're you're
comping you know nearly $30 trillion of
credit growth uh globally uh um kind of
in the last three quarters of 2020 uh in
terms of the refinancing need that we
see currently and that's just in terms
of the growth obviously you have you
know bills and things maturing across
the world Etc um so that's as U you know
we got there are many tens of trillions
of dollars of refinancing need that
needs to take place this year and uh and
tariffs certainly aren't helping that um
there's another way in which tariffs
cause problems to a liquidity
perspective so uh there's two ways in
our opinion at least according to our
analysis there are two ways in which
tariffs um drain liquidity one uh kind
of consistent with the um with the
questions implying is you have uh uh
what what would have been you know
household savings corporate profits and
and and foreign FX reserves uh those get
diverted from you know being those
things to you know uh winding up on the
US treasury's balance sheet in the form
of of Customs receipts so you're
essentially siphoning funds from the
private sector in the form that would
have been household savings corporate
profits and and and um and Fiat and and
sort Fiat FX reserves to the US Treasury
so that's obviously an erosion of of
liquidity from that perspective uh and
then you also have an erosion of
liquidity uh typically uh Vis the
currency markets typically uh what you
tend to see is that the current the the
country that the where the tariffs are
being administered to uh they tend to
devalue their currency to offset the um
offset the the price of the tariffs and
so if you think about um the potential
for tariffs particularly U you know
blanket tariffs on whole Industries or
blanket tariffs on whole countries the
possibility that the US dollar
strengthens to offset that uh is a
direct headwind for Global liquidity as
well so uh those are two specific ways
in which tariffs um drain liquidity
obviously if we have a significant
increase in capex here any a year where
we also have a you know historic
acceleration in debt refinancing demand
uh that's not going to be great from the
perspective of trying to prevent this
liquidity air pocket so uh all the more
reason Mr pal if you're listening for
you to step up and start supplying the
world with uh liquidity U um to ease
some of the pressure from the crowding
out that we continue to observe um you
know in the treasury market and how that
is um you know contributing to a
potential Global refinancing a pocket as
it continues to crowd out um you know
balance sheet capacity from the rest
